data.title: The Fed Made Its Third Cut Of The Year; Expectations Are Brightly Flashing
data.description:
For the last time in 2025, the Fed cut interest rates by a quarter of a percentage point. The target range for the federal funds rate now sits at 3.50-3.75%. Fed rate cuts can impact the markets and your investments in many ways. This applies to bonds, stocks and the strategy that you are using for your portfolio. Heading into the meeting, members of the Federal Open Market Committee pointed to inflation and signs of a weakening labor market as factors that could, and did, influence their decision.

When inflation rises, policymakers typically respond by raising the federal funds rate, which puts downward pressure on inflation by reducing consumer demand. When unemployment rises, policymakers try to boost the economy by lowering the inflation rate. Inflation remains above the Fed’s 2% target and has been ticking up this year. However, the unemployment rate has also risen recently, leaving officials in a quandary. In its FOMC minutes, the Fed acknowledged that inflation remains “somewhat elevated,” but also warned that unemployment could rise further. They are caught between a rock and a hard place.

According to the Fed’s statement, most officials think at least one more rate cut will be appropriate in 2026. Consumers could see interest rates on certain products inch down in anticipation of a cut. This, however, is pure speculation, as new members of the Fed will be appointed by the President in the upcoming year. After the cut was announced, the DJIA closed +497, while Treasury yields dipped. In the weeks leading up to this announcement, market prognosticators made a wide variety of market calls, and yesterday we got the actual answer.

Even with the release of the Fed’s latest projections, it’s too soon to say for certain whether the central bank will continue lowering rates next year. According to CME FedWatch, an additional 50 basis points in cuts by the end of 2026 are likely, but that could change depending on the state of the economy and by who is appointed to the Fed.

Changes to the federal funds rate can affect the rates offered on consumer loans like mortgages, auto loans, personal loans and credit cards. Exactly how interest rates are impacted, though, depends on the type of loan, the length of the loan and the purpose for which the loan is being used. If you have a loan with a fixed interest rate, your monthly payment won’t be affected by this latest Fed move, since your rate is already set. If you have a variable-rate loan or are shopping for a new loan, the Fed cut could help you get a slightly lower monthly payment. You might also see some lowering of interest rates on credit cards, but the American banks in general have not lowered rates regardless of the Fed’s prognostications. If you have money in a CD, you might soon earn less interest in those funds as banks adjust their savings rates even as they are keeping their borrowing rates quite high, in my opinion.

Stocks jumped and bonds dipped immediately following the Fed’s announcement, as I commented earlier, but we could see continued volatility as investors digest the commentary and forward guidance coming out of the meeting. One thing is for sure: if you have your money in a high-yield savings account, or a CD is coming due, you should shop the market to get the best rate possible. Different banks have widely divergent rates now. Rates vary from bank to bank, and just because your current bank had the best rate when you initially opened an account doesn’t mean that it still does.

This was the Fed’s last meeting of 2025, and it won’t meet again until January. In the intervening time, markets will closely watch the latest data and public remarks from policymakers, our politicians, our President and members of the Fed to understand whether more rate cuts are likely in 2026. Given President Trump’s recent pronouncements, I would expect more Fed cuts in the coming year. That is my opinion.

One of the next major reports to come out will be the November jobs report on December 16, followed by November’s consumer price index data release on December 18. Higher inflation typically makes it less likely that the Fed will cut rates, while a weaker labor market makes cuts more likely. Once these reports and other data are made public, we’ll be able to better sense what the economy and the markets may do in the forthcoming year.

While I am still in favor of investing for income, I also recognize that appreciation should be a part of your thinking when considering your investments. I also think the effects of AI will begin to wear off soon, as many strategists have already included the upcoming changes in the economy given the broader knowledge of what AI will do to the economy. My monthly income strategy will also help, as it provides new cash each month that can be invested based upon what is happening in different sectors of the markets. I remain cautious, but I am also more positive about our markets than I was several months ago.

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nvidia: H200 Export To China Is Mostly Optics, But The Strong Buy Case Isn't
data.description:
President Trump has approved the sale of Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) H200 chips to China. On paper, that is a move that should help Nvidia maintain and accelerate its AI leadership position. However, I believe that the approval of sales could be more symbolic rather than adding tangible upside to sales prospects for Nvidia. In this report, I discuss why I believe that is the case.

U.S. To Collect 25% Revenue Share On H200 Sales

Sales to China have been under scrutiny for some years, and as export regulations tightened, shipments of H20 chips were halted to China over security concerns. The U.S. has now technically approved sales of the H200, which is the base product from which the H20 chip tailored for the Chinese market was developed, exports on which it collects a 25% revenue share. Before shipments were halted earlier this year, Nvidia had $4.6 billion in H20 sales and $2.5 billion in quarterly sales that it could not deliver. This brings the quarterly figure to $7.1 billion. At a rate of 25%, the U.S. would collect almost $1.8 billion on these sales if sales to China would revive.

Technically, the approval of the H200 exports is an upgrade from the H20 for China, but it may come a little too late, as increasingly more strict export regulations for chip sales to China since 2022 have not slowed down Chinese capabilities but boosted chip innovation.

H20/H200 Chip Sales Revival To China Is Highly Uncertain

While the removal of the ban generally looks like a bullish sign for Nvidia, the reality might be that sales potential for the H20 or H200 is extremely small. In August, China warned Chinese companies against the use of chips from Nvidia and AMD and at this moment we have not seen any change in that stance. Any Chinese company interested in Nvidia’s chips has to carefully lay out why it does require chips from Nvidia and chips from domestic companies such as Huawei and Alibaba are not sufficient. What this essentially means to sales prospects is that while the U.S. ban may have been lifted, the Chinese ban has not been lifted. Unless there is any substantial change on Chinese approval for importing Nvidia chips, there is little to no upside for Nvidia driven by the H20/H200 chip.

The Positives Of U.S. Approval Of Advanced Chip Sales To China

If China does not become more flexible on Nvidia chip imports, it does not necessarily create a major issue for Nvidia. The company’s guidance already does not include any assumed sales for data center chips to China. Sales to China, however, would be a clear bonus if they do occur. The bigger positive is the access to the Chinese market. From here, the next step would essentially be to have a custom chip for China based on the Blackwell architecture. Realistically, it may take time before there actually is a ready-to-ship product with U.S. approval. So, at this time for Nvidia and perhaps even for any revenues for the U.S. the approval is only symbolic. The move may be less symbolic in trade context and provide an opening for constructive discussions on trade of which chips may be a part.

The Best Expectation Is To Not Expect Sales To China

To assess expectations for Chinese sales, we have to look at how estimates have developed for next year. We note that sales expectations have increased by less than a percent. EBITDA expectations increased 1.6% and free cash flow expectations improved by 2%. While these are positive revisions, they provide no indication that analysts are currently expecting any substantial sales for China. I believe that not including any sales to China to expectations is the correct one. That ship largely seems to have sailed. The key metrics above, however, show that the company has an impressive growth trajectory ahead with EBITDA margins around 64%-65% and strong sales with a sales CAGR of 37.7%. Free cash flow is expected to grow 43.8% annually making Nvidia a cash powerhouse.

The obvious risk for Nvidia and many companies leaning on AI expansion is the CapEx-heavy investment cycle. For many companies, the big question is where the cash is going to come from. That is primarily the case for OpenAI.

In my earnings review in November, I provided an updated price target and upgraded Nvidia stock to a Strong Buy. The stock has gained 0.7% since then, but it is not really a timeline on which I attach any substantial value to the stock price development. This article also does not serve the purpose to update the stock price targets, but since I did add the most recent estimates in the model it does not hurt to share those with those that are interested.

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The base case price target has increased fractionally from $241.02 to $241.90. For 2027, there has been a lift of around $6 per share to $379.76 and little over $7 per share to $487.12 for 2028. Taking the price target in between the peer group and the company’s median EV/EBTIDA, we get a $276.50 price target indicating around 50% upside when factoring in earnings for 2027 and $355 or 93% upside when keeping the earnings for 2028 in mind. So, at this point I do not see any reason to adjust my strong buy rating.

Conclusion: Don’t Expect Anything Big From China For Now

In theory, the approval of H200 chip sales to China is a positive for Nvidia and an upgrade for China from the bandwidth-limited H20 chip. However, for the moment I do not expect any substantial addition of sales for Nvidia as China has not signaled any change in stance on the purchase of chips from Nvidia. My fear for the H200 sales cycle is that the lift of the ban has come a little too late, and what China may be looking for is a chip based on the Blackwell architecture, which I believe is not going to happen any time soon. It does not change my bullish stance on Nvidia, but I believe the near-term benefit for Nvidia from the H200 export license to China may be extremely limited.

data.title: Oracle Joins Code Red Club
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Oracle (ORCL) just plummeted over 10% after Q2. A great quarter by many measures, but investors are becoming sceptical of the AI narrative.

Cloud revenue was up 34%, with Cloud Infrastructure up 68% and RPO exploding 438% year over year to $523 billion.

But with heavy capex and negative free cash flow, Oracle will need to raise huge amounts of debt to fund AI build-outs.

The key questions now are simple: will that record RPO convert to high-margin revenue quickly enough, is the balance sheet safe, and when does free cash flow re-emerge?

Ultimately, I do believe the sell-off offers a reasonably good entry point, though investors should be wary not to overexpose themselves to AI names.

Q2 Earnings

Let’s break down the headline numbers first.

Revenues increased to $16.1B, up 14% in YoY. This was driven by growth in the Cloud segment, which can be broken down into Cloud Applications and Cloud Infrastructure.

The exciting area is Cloud Infrastructure, which increased 68%, and should become the bulk of revenues if Oracle can execute on its promising backlog of AI contracts.

Operating income increased 10% YoY to $6.7 billion, with a non-GAAP operating margin of 42%.

The company also beat on EPS, but this was largely due to $2.7 billion pre-tax gain on the sale of Oracle’s stake in Ampere.

Insights From The Earnings Call

Now, let’s cover some interesting insights from the earnings call.

We no longer think it is strategic for us to continue designing, manufacturing and using our own chips in our cloud datacenters… We are now committed to a policy of chip neutrality where we work closely with all our CPU and GPU suppliers.

Source: Earnings Call

In other words, Oracle has abandoned in-house chip design and adopted a full pivot that should allow for greater agility and capital efficiency.

In terms of AI, Ellison succinctly laid out the value proposition for Oracle

Training AI models on public data is the largest, fastest-growing business in history. AI models reasoning on private data will be an even larger and more valuable business. Oracle databases contain most of the world's high-value private data.

Source: Earnings Call

And the upside is not just limited to the infrastructure buildout, management believes.

AI, of course, is a great OCI play, but it's also a broader software play for Oracle. It's driving growth in our applications, in our database businesses as well.

Source: Earnings Call

Why The Sell-Off?

So why is Oracle down +10%? It seems like just weeks ago, this was one of the hottest AI plays.

As always, though, narratives can quickly shift, especially in the face of cold, hard numbers that challenge them

Capex and Free Cash Flow

Trailing twelve-month capex has ballooned to $35.5 billion, while free cash flow is negative $13.2 billion.

Free cash flow as a percentage of net income has moved from +103% to -85%

Yes, this is part of a deliberate pivot to capital-intensive AI infrastructure build-out, but the market has priced this trade in recent weeks, especially as OpenAI undergoes its code red moment.

The Debt Concerns

Oracle’s PP&E has jumped from $43.5 billion to $67.9 billion since May, and non-current borrowings rose from $85.3 billion to $100.0 billion over the same period.

By many estimates, Oracle would need $100 billion of incremental capital to fulfil AI demand, something which the company’s management pushed back against in the earnings call.

We've read quite a few [analyst reports] that show an expectation of upwards of $100 billion for Oracle to go out and kind of complete these buildouts. And based on what we see right now, we expect we will need less, if not substantially less money raised than that amount…

Source: Earnings Call

But the fact that he had to address this explicitly tells you what the investors are worried about.

Is It Time To Buy The Dip?

This really comes down to your belief on three key questions:

  1. Will record RPO convert into revenue at attractive margins?
  2. Is the balance sheet strong enough to fund the build-out?
  3. When does free cash flow turn positive again?

If you believe Oracle can execute on those three, then an AI-infrastructure-driven sell-off is typically a buying opportunity.

If you think capex will need to stay at this level for years with only gradual margin expansion, the stock can stay under pressure.

Let’s go through each.

Will RPO Materialise?

RPO is the core of the bull case.

Who wouldn’t love this chart?

Oracle now expects $4 billion of additional revenue in FY27 versus prior estimates, driven by the new AI contracts.

And, to be fair, management is explicit that this isn’t “distant” backlog:

The vast majority of these bookings relate to opportunities where we have near-term capacity available, which means we can convert the added backlog to revenue sooner.

Source: Earnings Call

On Cloud Infrastructure specifically, capacity is now scaling at hyperscaler-like levels with 50% more GPU capacity delivered sequentially and more than 96,000 NVIDIA GB200 delivered into the Abilene, Texas supercluster

And according to the company multicloud database consumption is exploding, with demand increasing 817% year-over-year, which is very bullish for Oracle.

Is Debt Manageable?

Yes, Oracle is now a levered AI infrastructure play. But there are three mitigating factors:

Capex is heavily tied to contracted demand and the company repeatedly emphasised it only accepts contracts once land, power, chips and economics line up.Furthermore, multiple funding models reduce Oracle’s own capex burden since Oracle is not always paying for the GPUs itself:

Customers can actually bring their own chips… Similarly, we have different models… where they rent their capacity rather than selling their capacity. Both of these options enable Oracle to synchronize our payments with our receipts and borrow substantially less than most people are modeling.

Source: Earnings Call

The balance sheet is not “pristine”, but it still looks manageable if RPO converts as guided and capex normalises down from this spike.

Free Cash Flow: Structural Problem Or Investment Phase?

The ugly line in the release is the free cash flow table we showed above.

That looks alarming until you remember a few key points.

Oracle does not pay for data-centre leases (land, buildings, power) until they are delivered.

And management claims the period between capex outlay and revenue is “on the order of a couple of months”:

In other words, this should be a deliberate, front-loaded investment phase to stand up massive AI capacity for signed customers. If they execute, free cash flow should inflect as the current wave of regions and superclusters comes online and AI workloads stabilise into long-lived, high-utilisation contracts with 30–40% gross margins.

Final Thoughts

All in all, I think this is a buyable dip in Oracle. For an AI stock well-positioned in the market, the valuation, at 30x PE, is not outrageous anymore.

The key is to buy when the narrative is at its worst, and I think we are getting close to that point.

While there are risks, I do believe this is a reasonable and logical AI buildout that will eventually pay off.

data.title: Oracle: The AI Capex Blunder Explained
data.description:
Oracle Corporation’s (ORCL) post-earnings stock performance was a classic representation of a broader pattern that’s been emerging across the current earnings season. The stock’s pullback was a response to Oracle’s higher-than-expected capex outlook for fiscal 2026, which wasn’t matched with a corresponding growth uplift for the year.

Despite delivering multi-fold remaining performance obligation (“RPO”) growth, which reinforces its AI pipeline, the juxtaposition of rising capex intensity and tepid incremental growth is raising concerns over a tempered ROI trajectory ahead. This has likely emerged as a looming valuation multiple compression risk for Oracle, as investors’ reassess Oracle’s ability in driving backlog conversion into realized revenue without compromising its earnings profile. The latest earnings shocker has likely also resuscitated some fears that Oracle’s cloud infrastructure margins remain far lower than market expectations, which potentially implies a longer timeline to earnings accretion – a concern that triggered the stock’s extended selloff since reaching its peak in late September.

Oracle’s latest post-earnings pullback was similar to dynamics observed earlier this earnings season with hyperscaler peers Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta Platforms (META). Both companies have raised their full year AI capex outlooks to address near-term capacity bottlenecks, yet neither were able to deliver corresponding uplifts to their revenue guidance. The dynamic had overshadowed an otherwise robust earnings report from both companies. Meanwhile, Google’s (GOOG / GOOGL) valuation as handsomely rewarded, despite it raising its full year capex outlook for the second time this year, as the decision was accompanied by a corresponding growth and earnings uplift.

The pattern has emerged as a telling tale of what investors are looking for. It’s evident that AI momentum is becoming more differentiated, with greater scrutiny for credibility underpinned by a balanced growth and cost profile that drives earnings accretion. AI premiums are no longer easily rewarded to any and all names that participate in the thematic catalyst. Instead, investors are increasingly demanding tangible monetization visibility to justify accelerating growth investment cycles. This accordingly harbingers heightened scrutiny over companies that are simply spending ahead of demand and rely on backlog expansion without clear conversion timelines to profitable growth – a situation Oracle’s found itself in.

Taken together, we believe Oracle’s post-earnings pullback reflects a realignment of its valuation premium towards levels closer to the embedded fundamental outlook. However, the stock’s performance at currently levels still prices in substantial execution demands ahead, as Oracle’s credibility remains on the line. Unlocking a sustained recovery from current levels will likely require several consecutive quarters of better-than-expected cloud revenue growth and earnings expansion to substantiate value accretion from Oracle’s elevated capex intensity.

Elevated Capex: Demand Signal Undermined by ROI Uncertainty

Oracle’s raised capex guidance for fiscal 2026 was a shocker during its latest earnings update. This wasn’t because investors were unaware that the company’s been scaling its AI roadmap aggressively, but rather because the heightened capex outlook lacked a corresponding uplift to Oracle’s growth.

Specifically, Oracle reported 42% sequential growth in fiscal Q2 capex to $12 billion. The figure now represents 75% of its quarterly revenue, up from 57% observed in fiscal Q1. Management’s also raised fiscal 2026 capex to $50 billion, which represents a $15 billion increase from the previous $35 billion guide. The upward revision shouldn’t be entirely surprising, as management had previously embedded an upward bias to Oracle’s growth investment outlook during the fiscal Q1 earnings update:

But at this point, I'm looking at $35 billion for the year. And I think -- I mean, it could be a little higher, but I think -- and if it is higher, it's good news because it means more capacity has been handed over to me in terms of floor space.

Source: Oracle Q1 FY 2026 Earnings Call Transcript.

Yet the magnitude of increase has far outpaced expectations – especially given Oracle’s fiscal Q2 revenue underperformance and maintained FY 2025 growth guidance. And while management cites the $15 billion increase to Oracle’s full year capex outlook is a direct reflection of Oracle’s strong backlog growth, the limited corresponding revenue uplift is raising concerns about the ROI trajectory on its AI roadmap.

It's also increasing investors’ angst over Oracle’s funding capabilities ahead. Specifically, Oracle’s elevated capex spend amid its aggressive AI growth investment cycle has already weighed on the company’s free cash flows in recent quarters. The company’s reported free cash flow in the last 12 months (“LTM FCF”) has been negative for three consecutive quarters since Q4 FY 2025. The negative LTM FCF balance is also growing, despite Oracle’s accelerating topline, which further limits visibility over the company’s ability in scaling AI-related revenues.

Meanwhile, Oracle’s debt profile’s also on the rise. Total notes payable and other borrowings, which captures Oracle’s debt exposure, rose 18% sequentially, implying higher financing costs ahead. A deeper dive into the fiscal Q2 press release disclosures also show new debt was acquired in the quarter. Net proceeds from commercial paper and other short-term financing, alongside net proceeds from issuances of senior notes and term loan credit agreements totaled $18.8 billion in the first half of fiscal 2026. After eliminating cash flows from related financing activities during fiscal Q1, it implies Oracle had acquired more than $1.1 billion in new proceeds from commercial papers and other short-term financing, and $17.9 billion in new proceeds from issuances of senior notes and term loan credit agreements during fiscal Q2.

The company’s heightened capex outlook is raising concerns about potentially extended funding needs ahead as well, despite its already-ballooning debt profile. The scrutiny is corroborated by the rise in funding-specific questions fielded from analysts during Oracle’s latest fiscal Q2 earnings update, to which management’s reaffirmed their intentions to keep the company’s investment-grade credit rating with flexible financing models. Management’s also cited that Oracle’s funding needs for building out its AI strategy remains “substantially less” than what’s being observed in “a lot of analyst reports” in an effort to assuage investors’ angst:

Similarly, we have different models that we're working on with different vendors, where some vendors are actually very interested in the model where they rent their capacity rather than selling their capacity. And as you can imagine, that comes with different cash flow impacts that are favorable and reduce the overall borrowing needs and capital required for Oracle…we will use a range and a variety of those such that we minimize the overall cost of capital. As well as, in certain cases, we'll be raising our own funds. As part of that, I think, it's important that everyone understand that we're committed to maintaining our investment-grade debt rating… What I would say is we've been reading a lot of analyst reports, and we've read quite a few that show an expectation of upwards of $100 million for Oracle to go out and kind of complete these build-outs. And based on what we see right now, we expect we will need less, if not substantially less money raised than that amount to go and fund this build out.

Source: Oracle Q2 FY 2026 Earnings Call Transcript.

However, while the non-debt funding management arrangements – such relying on capacity rentals from vendors – could alleviate immediate capex outlays and borrowing needs, it also introduces variability to Oracle’s cost structure. Not only does this not fully address Oracle’s immediate concerns on funding its immediate cash burn run-rate and debt accumulation, but it also introduces greater uncertainties over its FCF accretion outlook.

It’s evident that Oracle’s elevated capex outlook alone isn’t the issue – especially as long-term demand remains robust and secured by its accelerating backlog. However, the lack of execution evidence that this spending trajectory is translating into accelerated revenue recognition and/or margin uplift remains a key risk overhang. With limited visibility into the returns profile of Oracle’s increasingly elevated investment cycle, the stock will likely remain prone to multiple compression risks ahead.

Delayed Acceleration: Limited Timing Visibility Into RPO Conversion

The timing mismatch between Oracle’s surging RPO and comparatively tepid revenue acceleration, alongside the recently raised capex outlook is another pressure point to consider.

Recall that management’s been repeatedly emphasizing Oracle’s efficient capital deployment model. Specifically, the company only deploys capex when the leased data center facilities are ready to come online. This should essentially imply rapid conversion of equipment spend into revenue-generating assets for Oracle and sped-up time-to-value for customers:

…the equipment CapEx is purchased very late in the data center production cycle, allowing us to quickly convert cash spent into revenues earned as we provision cloud services to our contracted and committed customers.

Source: Oracle Q2 FY 2026 Earnings Call Transcript.

Yet the latest earnings results highlights uncertainties around when Oracle’s accelerating backlog will convert into P&L.

To further justify its raised FY 2026 capex outlook, management’s disclosed that they now expect $4 billion in additional FY 2027 revenue on top of previous guidance. Based on disclosures from Oracle’s latest Analyst Presentation, management had called for $32 billion in Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (“OCI”) revenue for FY 2027, which implies 78% y/y growth. The additional $4 billion uplift to guided FY 2027 revenue would raise segment growth expectations to 100% y/y. Yet even with this uplift, OCI revenue growth would still trail the 433% y/y RPO growth by wide margins. This could suggest that Oracle’s become increasingly exposed to a longer realization runway on newly forged AI contracts due to a multitude of factors, including an extended weighted-average contract life and/or supply constraints.

We believe the latter is of greater risk, given management’s disclosed that the vast majority of bookings “relate to opportunities where [Oracle has] near-term capacity available” to convert into revenue sooner. This would align with broader hyperscaler observations, whereby the biggest monetization bottleneck is represented by supply constraints rather than demand. While this supports a favorable backdrop for continued RPO expansion, it also simultaneously risks a greater capex spend trajectory ahead to support procurements required to bring revenue-generating capacity online; delays to backlog conversion, which could push revenue recognition and ensuing FCF accretion further out into the future; and slower-than-expected cloud market share expansion against larger competitors like Microsoft Azure, Amazon Web Services (AMZN) and Google Cloud Platform (“GCP”).

This could represent a headwind to OCI’s intentions of capturing a greater share of impending IT spending intentions that prioritize cloud and AI infrastructure deployments. Although Oracle’s been increasingly relying on multi-cloud database deployments via integration with AWS, Azure and GCP, which is accelerating at a faster pace than peers per management, its absolute market share remains low. Based on a recent industry CIO survey, more than half of respondents have highlighted that they do not yet have workloads allocated to Oracle Cloud Infrastructure.

The combined risks of further capex elevation and share gain uncertainties are essentially raising execution challenges ahead for Oracle, especially considering intensifying competition and looming macro uncertainties that could shift enterprise IT budgets. For now, the company’s reported 68% y/y Cloud Infrastructure revenue growth, which is in line with the segment’s implied CAGR through FY 2030 per Oracle’s latest Analyst Presentation. Yet limited upside relative to this baseline, especially given Oracle’s raised capex trajectory, remains a lingering multiple compression risk, as it reduces the buffer for absorbing execution headwinds ahead.

Fundamental Considerations

Considering Oracle’s latest fiscal Q2 earnings results and forward outlook based on our foregoing analysis, we expect the company to finish FY 2026 with revenue growth of 14% y/y to $65.3 billion. We expect the cloud segment – primarily OCI – to remain Oracle’s long-term growth driver. This would be consistent with what’s been propelling Oracle’s surging RPO in recent quarters, and aligned with management’s expectations for OCI revenue to reach $144 billion by FY 2030.

*See here for enlarged graphs: Oracle_-_Supplemental_Graphs.pdf.

Meanwhile, our forecast predicts Oracle’s GAAP operating margins to remain largely stable through fiscal 2027, then expand more meaningfully starting fiscal 2028 when AI-related OCI deployments start to unlock greater operating leverage improvements. This is expected to drive more meaningful non-GAAP operating margin expansion by then, as sales costs and stock-based compensation as a percentage of revenue also start to decline with scaled OCI deployment.

Price Considerations

Our base case fundamental projections for Oracle yields an implied intrinsic value of $262 per share for the stock.

The price target is computed under the discounted cash flow (“DCF”) approach, and considers projections from the fundamental forecast discussed above. The analysis includes consideration of a $1.1 trillion terminal value, which is derived from the application of a 1.5% perpetual growth rate on Oracle’s projected FY 2030 EBITDA. The 1.5% perpetual growth rate reflects the anticipated pace of long-term economic growth across Oracle’s core operating regions, and imitates the expected rate at which its steady-state cash flows, as proxied by terminal EBITDA over the forecast period, will expand. Total cash flow projections over the forecast period, including the terminal value, is discounted at an 8.8% WACC, which is in line with Oracle’s capital structure and risk profile.

Admittedly, the base case price target of $262 per share represents upside of 37% from the stock’s currently traded levels of about $191 per share (December 11 open). However, based on our foregoing analysis, we believe significant execution remains on the line until Oracle can deliver a sustained recovery. Its price at current levels after the post-earnings pullback now adequately reflects Oracle’s exposure to imminent downside risks. And given limited immediate upside catalysts ahead, as Oracle works on delivering execution consistency, we remain incrementally cautious on its outlook and assign a neutral rating on the stock.

Final Thoughts

Oracle’s latest post-earnings blunder reinforces expectations that the AI-driven market momentum is now being highly scrutinized for substantiable underlying fundamental support. Investors are no longer appeased by the robust demand outlook underpinned by the long-term secular AI transformation, as represented by strong RPO growth. Instead, tangible conversion to revenue growth and earnings accretion is required to justify increased capital intensity, highlighting investors’ demand for improved visibility into the long-term AI ROI roadmap. And based on Oracle’s latest results, it’s evident that the company’s yet to establish credibility on that front.

Specifically, the company’s raised capex outlook, with limited corresponding uplift to its growth trajectory, has only exacerbated earlier concerns about Oracle’s underperforming AI margins, adding pressure on the stock’s recovery prospects. The muted near-term revenue uplift also contrasts Oracle’s outsized RPO expansion, highlighting risks of a protracted supply bottleneck and requirement for further capex ahead to address imminent demand.

Taken together, we believe Oracle’s latest post-earnings pullback is a warranted recalibration to better align its valuation with the underlying business’ fundamental outlook. However, the catalyst for a sustained recovery from here will remain dependent on execution consistency and restored credibility, which could take several quarters to materialize.

data.title: The First Crack In Nvidia's CUDA
data.description:
This feels like taking Warren Buffett’s advice about reading obscure trade material and actually putting it to work. On its technical blog in early December, Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) announced its “largest and most comprehensive update to the CUDA platform since it was invented two decades ago.” Outside of developer forums, this has barely made any headlines. Yet this update may be the first clear sign that Nvidia is quietly responding to the shift OpenAI (OPENAI) set in motion against the most critical aspect of its CUDA moat. That shift is a free open-source project called Triton that makes GPU programming radically simpler.

Triton operates as what engineers call a compiler, and in terms of architecture it sits a layer above CUDA. As seen in the image, Triton lets developers avoid writing CUDA directly, and I discuss later in the article how it functions to erode the CUDA moat.

The Update Almost Everyone Missed

The headline features a new tile-based programming model but let’s break that down. Imagine directing a marching band with 200 musicians. The old CUDA way was to give instructions to the musicians individually. Let’s say the instructions are like the one below.

Instruction - "Trumpet player #47, take 3 steps forward on beat 4."

The tile-based programming model is like saying “Brass section, form a triangle” and letting someone else figure out who moves where.

In this analogy, the marching band is the GPU, the 200 musicians are the threads, and the brass section is a tile or a block. A thread is a worker that executes the instruction and the instruction itself is called the kernel.

Triton has used a block-based (or tile-based) model since 2021. In 2022, Nvidia moved its hardware to tiles with the Hopper H100 GPUs. But the software lagged the hardware and has allowed Triton to gain a lot of ground.

Dissecting the CUDA Moat

To understand why Triton has gained ground, it is important to dissect the CUDA moat into its three reinforcing layers – the library ecosystem, tooling, and human capital.

The library ecosystem is best visualized by thinking about buying a table from IKEA. You get pre-cut wood, pre-drilled holes and screws that fit perfectly. All you have to do is assemble them. Because it has been crafted over a period of two decades, CUDA’s mature library repository is highly optimized. For instance, with the Hopper architecture Nvidia introduced a transformer engine in it’s cuDNN (DNN - deep neural networks) library that accelerated large language model, or LLM, training.

In contrast, building without this library is like going to a lumber yard. Here is a pile of logs and a saw. Good luck!

The tooling advantage with CUDA is less talked about and underestimated because it is highly developer oriented. Nvidia’s Nsight systems is a debugging tool that lets you see exactly what’s happening inside the chip. In a large training cluster, a software bug that causes GPU idle time can cost thousands of dollars per hour. Nvidia’s tooling allows engineers to diagnose faults quickly and minimizes downtime. The lack of tooling ecosystems was a frequent source of irritation with some of the other frameworks like Advanced Micro Devices’ (AMD) ROCm. I must add that AMD released certain new features last month with a section focus on debugging.

The most enduring source of the moat is human capital. CUDA has been the default stack for parallel computing (GPUs) in universities and research institutions. This has created a massive talent pool of engineers who are natively fluent in CUDA. Why is it enduring? Think about it, companies hire CUDA-proficient engineers because they are abundant. These engineers then advocate for Nvidia hardware precisely because it aligns with their expertise. Shifting to an alternative hardware would require re-skilling the workforce at a great cost.

How Triton Attacks the Human Capital Layer

As we saw earlier, Triton is a compiler that converts code written in Python to an optimized code for any GPU, whether it is Nvidia, AMD, or Intel (INTC). Compared to maybe a few thousand CUDA experts, there are probably a million developers proficient in Python. So, it widens the net for who can produce GPU performant code.

There is, however, a performance trade-off. PyTorch’s (an open-source deep learning library founded by Meta Platforms (META)) tests last year found that Triton delivers about 70% to 80% of the performance of hand-optimized CUDA. So the key question is why is it still a threat?

Because it dramatically accelerates development. Triton effectively bypasses the static library ecosystem by enabling the on-the-fly generation of custom, high-performance kernels. At the 2025 PyTorch Conference in October, IBM (IBM) showed how they delivered “state-of-the-art performance across GPU platforms with a single code base, without involving hand-written CUDA or HIP kernels”. The practical implication for hyperscalers is they can have their regular ML researchers write performant GPU code instead of specialized CUDA engineers. As one developer put it, "It has allowed us to write performant kernels faster than authoring them using vendor specific APIs."

This shift is reflected in the near-ecosystem level project numbers for Triton. It’s repository on github now shows ~75,000 software projects reliant on it.

A Coordinated Attack

The companies backing Triton reads like a who’s who of Nvidia’s customers and competitors. They have a strong cost incentive to create a credible threat against Nvidia.

While there has been a lot of news around Google (GOOGL), Meta has arguably played a major role in the acceleration of Triton. As a contributor to the PyTorch project, Meta made Triton the default compiler for code generation in 2023. Essentially this acts like a trojan horse as every PyTorch user is now implicitly using Triton whether they realize it or not.

Google shields its TPU chips and has Triton working in the backend only for GPUs. For TPUs it has a custom backend called Mosaic. Microsoft (MSFT) has similarly integrated Triton to its Azure infrastructure and hosts the annual Triton conference.

Because the most compelling case for Triton is that it makes hardware portable, it goes without saying that AMD is a major backer and Intel’s involvement is new but active.

Nvidia's Strategic Concession

This brings us back to the Nvidia update. It is in a way an admission that Triton's block-based model is superior for modern AI. The problem is the CUDA update only takes away one of Triton's appeals - ease of use. The primary economic problem for hyperscalers is the vendor lock-in, which Nvidia has every incentive to keep.

On the other hand, Triton is very good at what it does but it's domain is restricted to deep learning for now. Some of the more complex algorithms (like Fast Fourier Transforms) have been developed over two decades. So one scenario is we could have a market share fragmented by domains. The more likely scenario is Triton is continuously upgraded and its deficits narrow over a period of time.

Economic Implications of a Middleware like Triton

The Triton threat is ultimately economic. It shifts the market from a “hardware + software” (NVIDA + CUDA) bundle to a modular market where hardware is interchangeable.

For companies like Meta or Microsoft, the ability to buy a chip that is 30% cheaper and has 2x the memory translates to millions in savings per training cycle. Triton makes those alternatives viable.

Cloud providers also dislike being beholden to a single supplier that sells at a very high margin. The presence of Triton allows a system like Azure to offer tiered pricing with minimal disruption. An example would be a premium pricing for Nvidia hardware and a cost-optimized standard for others, all executed with the same customer code. Efficiency players like Databricks are already utilizing Triton to run customer training jobs on available AMD hardware as they can lower costs for users and also improve their own margins. So I am not sure how this can be reversed even if Nvidia’s platform is easier to code.

Nvidia’s TTM net margins are also quite high at 53%. Let’s assume a $300 billion revenue in 2026 based on the backlog mentioned in Q3 earnings. That translates to a net income of $159 billion and forward P/E ratio of 28.24. In light of the supply squeeze in the industry, I don’t see these developments being reflected in the numbers at least until the second half of 2026. But I’d be very surprised if margin compression does not seep into the commentary in the Q3 results next year. I am therefore very cautious on this stock and maintain a Hold rating for now.

Conclusion

I might be early on this, but I think the emergence of Triton has parallels to the mobile phone market pre-Android. Before Android, every phone maker had a proprietary OS. Android became the middleware that ran on Samsung, LG, or Motorola. Similarly, Triton is the Android of AI Compute. It allows "apps" (PyTorch models) to run on phones (GPUs) from Nvidia, AMD, Intel and maybe custom silicon in the future. It doesn't threaten the CUDA moat by being better at what CUDA does. Instead, it may make CUDA irrelevant for a vast majority of AI developers. And by doing that, it is slowly dismantling the lock-in of the most valuable company in the world.

data.title: Buy Confluent For The Attractive Arbitrage Spread, Buy IBM For Potential Hybrid Cloud Dominance
data.description:
Earlier this week, International Business Machines Corporation (IBM) made a significant move by acquiring Confluent, Inc. (CFLT) in an all-cash deal. From Confluent’s perspective, the deal offers an attractive low-risk opportunity to secure an annualized return of 6.76%, representing a 3.13% premium to the risk-free 6-month treasury.

As for IBM, acquiring Confluent offers a path to accelerated growth thanks to Confluent’s perfect fit with its software stack including Red Hat OpenShift, HashiCorp, and watsonx, as the data streaming layer. In fact, Confluent’s cloud-agnostic nature positions it to become the nervous system of IBM’s entire hybrid cloud and AI stack. Meanwhile, integrating Confluent into IBM’s existing software products allows IBM to capitalize on the growing demand for data streaming platforms and presents major opportunities for upselling and cross-selling Confluent’s products throughout its large customer base.

Given IBM’s go-to-market scale, international footprint, and high penetration of Fortune 500 enterprises, Confluent could become a major contributor to IBM’s EPS by the fifth year post deal. As is, my estimates show that Confluent can grow its revenues 2.1 times by the fifth year post deal, while achieving a 26% EBIT margin and a 20.8% profit margin by then. In light of these factors, I’m rating Confluent as a buy for the arbitrage premium and IBM as a buy for its potential hybrid cloud dominance and growth acceleration after acquiring Confluent.

Confluent: Attractive Arbitrage Premium

On December 8th, IBM agreed to acquire Confluent for $31 per share, implying an $11 billion enterprise value, in an all-cash deal to accelerate its pivot into an AI-centric software and data infrastructure company. The deal is expected to close in mid-2026 and will be funded with IBM’s cash on hand, which was valued at $14.9 billion as of the end of Q3.

Concurrently, IBM entered into a voting agreement with Confluent’s largest shareholders, representing 62% of the voting power, according to which, each of them agreed to vote in favor of the deal and against any alternative transaction. This suggests that the likelihood of the deal receiving shareholder approval is extremely high.

On the regulatory side, the deal is unlikely to face scrutiny due to IBM’s smaller cloud infrastructure footprint compared to hyperscalers and Confluent’s technology being built on open-source Apache Kafka. With shareholder support secured and a high probability of the deal going through, I believe Confluent presents an attractive arbitrage play.

At Confluent’s current share price of $30, the spread between IBM’s offer and its share price is $1, or a 3.33% absolute return. Assuming 180 days to the deal closing, since it is expected in mid-2026, the current spread implies an annualized return of 6.76%, whereas the risk free 6-month treasury yield is 3.63%. This means that arbitrageurs would receive a 3.13% premium over the risk-free rate for taking on the low-risk of the deal closing, in my opinion.

With that in mind, the attractiveness of Confluent’s merger arbitrage could further increase after the latest 25 bps rate cut. In response, the 6-month treasury yield declined from 3.70% to 3.63%, and could further decline to a range between 3.5-3.6%, in my opinion, to reflect the lower WACC in the financial system. In that case, the arbitrage premium could widen to 3.16-3.26%, further supporting the buy case for Confluent post the acquisition announcement.

IBM: Unlocking Confluent’s Value Offers A Path To Meaningful Growth Acceleration

From IBM’s perspective, acquiring Confluent fits perfectly with its transformation into a software-centric company that is focused on hybrid cloud and AI. Confluent’s strategic value comes from its position as the commercial steward of Apache Kafka, having been founded by Kafka’s creators, and its full data streaming platform that supports connectors, governance, stream processing through Apache Flink, and streaming agents.

At the same time, Confluent’s platform is cloud agnostic as it is able to gather scattered data across multiple clouds in real-time and unify them into a single data stream to feed AI models and agents. This is especially meaningful since large enterprises typically run workloads across their data centers and multiple cloud providers.

These capabilities mean that Confluent could provide the continuous real-time data streams needed to support the AI agents offered by watsonx, enhance HashiCorp’s event-driven automation capabilities, and simplify real-time data movement of applications built on Red Hat OpenShift across on-premises and public clouds. In this way, Confluent could significantly improve IBM’s software offerings by acting as the real-time data nervous system that connects IBM’s entire hybrid cloud and AI stack.

In addition to the product and strategic fit, Confluent paves the way for IBM to accelerate its high-margin software segment’s growth due to the combination of growing demand for real-time data streaming and processing and IBM’s go-to-market scale. Demand for data streaming has never been higher due the ever increasing growth of data, applications, and AI adoption driving IT complexity. IDC estimates that more than 1 billion new logical applications are expected to emerge by 2028 to drive outcomes and productivity in operations.

As these applications and AI agents require access to connected and trusted data in real-time, 89% of IT leaders consider data streaming platforms critical to achieving their data-related goals, with 86% ranking data streaming as a critical strategic priority, per Confluent’s 2025 Data Streaming Report. This demand is expected to drive total enterprise AI spend to more than $800 billion by 2029, more than 3 times 2024 spend.

Despite this favorable market environment, Confluent hasn’t been able to aggressively gain share of the Apache Kafka unit TAM estimated at more than 150 thousand users as a standalone company. With 6500 customers, Confluent only serves 4.3% of the total Kafka installed base, and despite serving 40% of the Fortune 500, it only has 234 customers with an ARR higher than $1 million, representing a meager 3.6% of its customer base.

Confluent’s low high-value customer base relative to its total customer base is a direct result of its lower profile compared to an established player like IBM and its strategy of targeting customers in their early stages and growing along them as their consumption of its products increases over time. In my opinion, Confluent being part of IBM could unlock its true value thanks to IBM’s global presence and high penetration rate of the Fortune 500.

IBM already serves 93% of the Fortune 500 and has operations in more than 170 countries. This market presence allows IBM to increase Confluent’s penetration of high-value customers globally, especially since it intends to to create a smart data platform that allows enterprises to scale generative AI across hybrid cloud by building a data layer through Confluent on top of its existing Red Hat OpenShift, HashiCorp, and watsonx offerings.

Through this strategy, IBM can help increase enterprises’ consumption of Confluent’s products, as well as introducing its integrated stack to enterprises modernizing their infrastructures through its consultancy, IBM Consulting. This can be achieved by moving clients from old batch processing to real-time streaming as well as utilizing Confluent’s library of pre-built connectors to integrate client’s legacy systems with its modern cloud applications. This could potentially result in larger multi-year deals for IBM consulting, providing another tailwind to IBM’s growth over the coming years.

Confluent’s EPS Contribution: 5-Year Forecast

From a financial perspective, IBM expects the Confluent acquisition to be accretive to adjusted EBITDA in the first year and to free cash flow in the second year post deal close. IBM also expects to to deliver run-rate synergies of $500 million following the acquisition by leveraging its go-to-market scale and optimizing G&A costs.

On a YTD basis, Confluent reported $852 million in revenues, including $818 million in high-margin, 78%, subscription revenue. However, the company reported an operating loss of $281 million over that period representing a -33% operating margin. The main contributors to Confluent’s non-profitability despite its high gross margin are its high sales and marketing costs and SBC to retain its talent. Over that period, sales and marketing costs were $432.7 million, representing 50.8% of its revenues, and SBC stood at $295.7 million, representing 34.7% of its revenues.

Confluent also exited Q3 with 21.3 million unvested RSUs holding an average fair value of $25.30, which will be converted into IBM RSUs post deal close, whereas all vested RSUs will be settled for cash, per the deal’s structure. This means that IBM will have to report around $538.7 million in SBC related to these unvested RSUs once Confluent is consolidated within its business, the bulk of which are likely to be reported over the first 2 years post deal close.

Given IBM’s go-to-market scale and the potential cost synergies, I expect Confluent to start impacting IBM’s EPS positively starting from the second year post deal close as my 5-year model will show.

Top-Line Forecast

As mentioned earlier, IBM’s go-to-market scale, high penetration of Fortune 500 enterprises, and its global footprint are likely to boost Confluent’s customer growth and expand customers’ consumption due to its ability to cross sell and upsell Confluent’s products along with its Red Hat OpenShift, HashiCorp, and watsonx offerings. This ability is likely to increase high value customers’ weight of Confluent’s total customers as well as increase customers’ consumption of Confluent’s products.

At 6500 customers, Confluent has added 820 customers since Q3 2024, however, as a result of the aforementioned factors, I’m estimating Confluent to reach 8500 customers in the first year post the IBM acquisition, and then expanding by 1000 customers annually through the fifth year post acquisition, reaching 13 thousand.

As for customer spending, Confluent currently generates around $44 thousand in quarterly subscription revenue per customer based on its Q3 subscription revenue of $286.3 million and a customer count of 6500. I’m forecasting this figure to expand by 10% in years 1 and 2 post deal close and decelerate to 8% in years 3 and 4 and 7% in year 5 post deal close. My forecast assumes higher value deals with Fortune 500 enterprises and higher consumption driven by infrastructure modernization through IBM Consulting.

Moreover, Confluent’s services segment, which provides professional and education services, currently has an annualized run rate of around $45 million as this segment generated $33.9 million in revenue YTD in FY 2025. Assuming no growth from this segment, I’m estimating Confluent’s revenues over the 5-year period following the deal close as follows.

My revenue estimates imply Confluent’s revenues to increase 2.1 times over the 5-year period post deal close. While this could appear aggressive, it is achievable considering that IBM was able to grow Red Hat’s revenues from $3 billion before acquiring it in mid-2019 to $6.5 billion in FY 2024, as shared in IBM’s 2025 Investor Day presentation.

This estimated revenue growth driven by higher value customer growth would reduce Confluent’s exposure to its negative gross margin services segment, which would drive gross margin expansion. On a YTD basis, Confluent reported a 74.1% gross margin, with its subscription segment reporting a 78% gross margin and the services segment reporting a -19.1% gross margin.

Accordingly, I’m estimating Confluent’s gross margin in the first year post deal to reach 75% and expand 1 percentage point annually to reach 79% in the fifth year post deal, leading to the following gross profit estimates.

I expect the 79% gross margin to be a ceiling for Confluent in following years due to growing cloud hosting costs as customers’ consumption of its products increase.

Bottom-Line Forecast

Moving on to the bottom-line, R&D represents the most crucial cost for Confluent to continue innovating solutions and maintain its leadership in data streaming. Year-to-date, Confluent reported $359.8 million in R&D costs, representing 42.2% of its revenues. On that note, IBM’s Senior Vice President, Software and Chief Commercial Officer, Rob Thomas, stated that IBM intends to grow Confluent’s R&D spend to accelerate the new business unit’s growth and its crucial nature to IBM’s software stack.

However, I’m estimating these increased investments to be more efficient post deal due to IBM’s ability to drive operating leverage within its acquired businesses. As such, I’m estimating R&D costs to represent 35% of revenues in the first year post deal and reach 25% in the fifth year post deal, which would see R&D spend increase 48% over that period.

That said, IBM’s expansive sales teams and its 93% penetration rate of Fortune 500 enterprises allows it to reduce Confluent’s sales and marketing costs as a percentage of revenue significantly, especially when taking its consultancy business into consideration. Under IBM, Confluent’s financial, legal, and HR teams can be absorbed into IBM’s existing teams, especially since its large scale allows it to process these functions at a lower cost than Confluent.
