Assuming a 20% tax rate and considering IBM’s diluted share count of 948.9 million, my 5-year model estimates Confluent to start contributing positively to IBM’s EPS in the second year post deal, while generating a 26% EBIT margin and contributing $0.77 to IBM’s EPS in the fifth year post deal. These estimates are consistent with IBM’s M&A rationale, shared in its 2025 Investor Day presentation, targeting a 20% PTI margin in the fifth year post acquisitions.

Risks

The main risk to my bullish thesis on both Confluent and IBM is the acquisition deal getting delayed or blocked by regulators. For Confluent, any delay beyond mid-2026 would negatively impact the arbitrage premium which may not make it as attractive as it currently is, while the deal getting blocked could see the stock plummet near its pre-acquisition levels.

As for IBM, the deal getting blocked would represent a massive blow to its software segment's potential growth due to Confluent’s ability to improve its offerings’ value proposition and growing its topline through upselling and cross-selling Confluent’s data streaming platform to existing enterprise customers.

IBM also faces risks of integrating Confluent into its operations which could delay the expected cost synergies, and thus, delaying Confluent’s positive contribution to IBM’s EPS. Finally, IBM could face risks while optimizing Confluent’s SG&A costs either due to IBM consulting failing to integrate Confluent into its sales funnel or excessive cost cuts that could hinder Confluent’s innovation pace and developer relationships which represent the core of its value.

Conclusion

In summary, Confluent presents an attractive opportunity for arbitrageurs with its current arbitrage premium over the 6-month treasury, especially with the fed’s latest rate cut, given the high likelihood of the IBM acquisition going through. As for IBM, acquiring Confluent could rapidly accelerate its growth thanks to the growing demand for data streaming and IBM’s potential to unlock Confluent’s true value with its go-to-market scale. Moreover, Confluent could help improve the value proposition of IBM’s software stack with its streaming capabilities, potentially driving demand for watsonx’s platform.

From a financial perspective, combining Confluent’s value proposition with IBM’s scale, high penetration of Fortune 500 enterprises, and global presence creates opportunities to drive high-value customers to Confluent’s platform and expand their consumption of its products. At the same time, the potential SG&A synergies could see Confluent start contributing positively to IBM’s EPS starting from the second year post deal, while reaching a 26% EBIT margin and a 20.8% profit margin in the fifth year post deal.

data.title: XFLT: We May Not Have Found The Bottom Yet In This High Yield CEF
data.description:
In my continuing search for income investments that are appropriate for retirees or someone approaching retirement, I am constantly on the lookout for opportunities to take advantage of tactical buying situations in high yield income funds. Back in April 2024, I suggested funding your retirement with an income fund that yielded 14% at the time and a discounted DRIP (dividend reinvestment policy) program that could potentially add to the income generating potential of an investment in the fund known as the XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT).

That article turned out to be not one of my better calls in the past couple of years. A few months after that article was published, the Fed started to lower interest rates (beginning in September 2024) and XFLT was forced to cut the monthly distribution in December 2024. Then, in June of this year, XFLT cut the distribution again, this time from $0.077 to $0.07.

Furthermore, the NAV declined from a 52-week high of $6.70 a year ago to just $5.43 as of 12/9/25. The market has punished the stock even further by sending the market price to a near 52-week low discount of almost -15% versus a slight premium to NAV a year ago as shown on CEFConnect.

As a result, the one-year total return of XFLT is a miserable -20%. A very disappointing result for investors who bought into the fund a year ago. However, XFLT may have found a bottom and could be a potential winner going forward. For that reason, I rate XFLT a Hold as we approach the end of 2025. However, additional interest rate cuts could lead to another distribution cut in 2026, and if that happens a further decline in the market price is likely.

As Kim Flynn, Partner and President of XFLT stated on the December Q3 2025 earnings call, lower interest rates have also been a benefit to the fund in terms of lower leverage costs:

Now as rates have come down, we've benefited from lower leverage expenses because our leverage -- a large part of our leverage is floating rate. And so we wanted to highlight that it's been a period where there's been a lot of change in the year ended 9/30. We're remaining steady with our monthly distributions, our most recent 3 monthly distribution declarations were at $0.07 per share, and we continue to manage distributions as we have in the past.

However, another CEF that is similar to XFLT in its holdings that includes mostly CLO debt (while XFLT is about 50/50 CLOs and senior loans) is Eagle Point Income fund (EIC). I last wrote about EIC in November 2023 when interest rates were still on the rise and at that time I suggested buying the fund that had delivered 15% annual returns and was still soaring.

However, in the last year EIC has also delivered a dismal total return of -20% and has cut the dividend dramatically, from $0.20 per share to $0.13 in July, and then again to $0.11 as of January 2026. I suspect that XFLT may also be forced to cut its distribution again in 2026.

XFLT Overview and Review of Holdings

When I covered XFLT back in August 2023, I wrote about how a fund like XFLT with its policy to DRIP (reinvest shares) at a discount to NAV could enhance your income stream, helping to generate a river of cash in retirement. At that time, XFLT had just increased the dividend.

Since I last wrote about XFLT, the fund managers announced an increase in the distribution in May of this year by 16%, from a previous dividend of $0.07 to $0.085 monthly, now yielding 15% annually. And if you elect to participate in the DRIP and your broker (I use Fidelity) supports the discounted DRIP, you can benefit from an additional increase of up to 5% per month by reinvesting those dividends rather than taking them as cash.

A snapshot of the fund from the website shows that the current NAV as of 12/9/25 (unlike other CLO funds, XFLT updates NAV daily) was $5.43. The closing price of $4.62 represents a discount of about -15% and a distribution yield of 18%. Leverage of 38.66% is on the high side and contributes to the downside return dynamics, however, as stated on the earnings call the fund managers are working to lower leverage costs as rates come down.

The asset allocation as of 9/30/25 as shown on the fund website is generally 50/50, weighted almost equally between CLOs and senior loans, with senior secured first lien loans at about 46% of the total portfolio value, followed by 36.8% in CLO Equity and 13.5% in CLO Debt.

The CLO Equity allocation has mostly contributed to the NAV decline over the past few months. Nearly all CLO Equity funds including OXLC, ECC, OCCI, CCIF, and SPMC have also seen rapid declines in their NAV due to compressed spreads and lower valuations for the equity tranches of CLOs.

On the December XFLT earnings call, Senior Portfolio Manager Lauren Law addressed that very important topic of the decline in NAV.

XFLT invest in broadly syndicated leveraged loans directly, CLO BB mezzanine tranches and of course, CLO equity. The loan market in total performed well in the third quarter. XFLT's loan allocation did as well. We actually did better than the market. Single B rated loans were the best performing portion of the loan market and XFLT is significantly overweight that rating cohort. The same was true for BB CLO tranches and the trust holdings of them.

CLO equity on the other hand, as I mentioned, had another tough quarter of performance in Q3. And while there's no index to quote CLO equity returns, many sell-side observers do post estimates. As far as we can tell, the trust equity performance did exceed the estimated performance of the market by a fairly good degree as well. However, it was a challenging period for CLO equity and the trust equity allocation was a drag on fund total return relative to its loan and BB mezz.

Regarding the tightened spreads, which affect the income generation of the XFLT portfolio, Ms. Law also addressed that topic with some helpful detail. This slide from the Q3 2025 investor presentation shows the tightening spread in 2025 that widened briefly after the April tariff tantrum.

Yes, spread tightening has been a continued challenge for the market this year. To say it very simply as to how this impacts XFLT, spread tightening does pressure the income earned by the trust. It pressures the income we earn on the loans and it pressures the income we earn on CLO equity. Tight and tightening spreads have been a continuing theme. They have weighed on CLO equity performance. Broadly syndicated leveraged loans benefit from 6 months of call protection. And that just means a borrower cannot reduce their interest rate in the first 6 months of the life of a loan. CLO liabilities, on the other hand, can only be refinanced lower every 2 years as they are structured with 2 years of call protection.

When asked if the market is near a floor in terms of CLO equity arbitrage and loan spreads, Ms. Law was optimistic in her comments regarding the outlook for 2026. Current spreads are at historically tight levels not seen since pre-2020 largely due to ongoing market uncertainty leading to a period of “technical imbalance.” An increase in M&A activity is expected to occur in Q1 2026 which should help to stimulate new loan issuance leading to wider spreads.

The forward calendar contains the largest ever LBO announced. That will be launching into the market very early in Q1. There are other large announced transactions that will follow it, and all signs point to increased M&A activity in addition to what is already announced. And so all of that is to say that I think spreads, while they may tick lower before they move higher I think, are very, very close to a floor and that should lead to a situation where CLO equity arbitrage is not going to get worse from here.

Looking at a longer-term view of XFLT we can see that the fund is approaching its all-time low NAV (when it hit $4.49 in March 2020) and now trades at a historically low discount to NAV of about -15%. However, given the fact that the economy is not currently in a recession, nor is there significant fear around credit risk other than the recent “cockroaches in the coal mine” that Howard Marks referred to in his recent memo, my prediction is that NAV will begin to stabilize near the current level and the price will begin to move back up as the discount closes.

The only time since inception that XFLT has traded at a lower discount was briefly in December 2018, and then in March 2020, and it quickly recovered afterward each time. Will this time be different?

In their Q3 2025 investor presentation, the company includes a slide showing previous CLO BB Debt Drawdowns and the recovery time for each. This slide does not include the latest one that occurred in Q4 (and which we are still experiencing) because it was based on results through 9/30/25, but it does offer encouraging insights based on recent history.

When asked about managing the risk of CLO equity in the fund’s portfolio and the outlook for 2026, Lauren Law explained that the two important factors they can control include 1) resets and refinancing activity, and 2) selection of active, best-in-class CLO managers.

The one main driver of CLO equity returns is the value of the cash flow stream earned by the equity. And as discussed, this has been under pressure as the income on loans has come down, spreads have repriced more quickly than CLO liabilities. And so to manage that element of return, we reset and refinance actively, which means we are targeting the equity we buy to be able to generate value from this reset and refinancing activity and then we push for that activity to occur as quickly as possible upon the expiration of non-call periods.

So a lot of our management of our equity book has resulted around picking managers, who actively manage credit risk and actively manage it successfully. Our views of managers can change over time. We are constantly analyzing recent performance, extended periods of performance and trying to anticipate which managers are going to continue to perform and which may not and trading our book accordingly.

Conclusion: Further Rate Cuts Could Cause Continued CLO Equity Pressure

Although they danced around this issue to some degree, preferring to focus on the good points in the XFLT fund portfolio that involve strong cash flows from senior loans and positive CLO Debt fundamentals, the elephant in the room remains CLO Equity.

Now that the Fed has announced yet another rate cut for 2025, loan spreads remain tight and may continue to put pressure on the CLO Equity tranches in the coming months unless we see a sudden pickup in new loan issuance associated with an increase in M&A activity in Q1 2026. That scenario does appear to be what the XFLT portfolio managers are counting on and if that is what happens then we could indeed start to see some improvement in CLO Equity assets.

With my mildly optimistic view of the market outlook for 2026, I do believe that we may have seen the bottom in the NAV of XFLT, however, the market price could remain discounted for quite some time until investors are convinced that a real recovery in CLO Equity has begun.

data.title: SPHQ: An Inside Look At The Quality Factor-Based ETF
data.description:
The Invesco S&P 500® Quality ETF (SPHQ) is one of the ETFs that come to mind when people talk about quality factor-based solutions. The reason is clear: although a gap is opening in total performance compared to pure S&P 500 replicating solutions, SPHQ remains a solution that has proven competitive compared to its peers. The selection process of the index that SPHQ replicates seems to be able to look well into the future, without forgetting the current quality of the companies it selects. And if at this level everything sounds very good, the question still arises spontaneously: how is this ETF built? But above all, what does it do, and for whom? But let's go in order.

The Invesco S&P 500 Quality ETF: An Introduction & Definition

SPHQ is an ETF offered by Invesco that replicates the S&P 500 Quality Index. This index selects the 100 companies with the highest quality scores within the S&P 500, on which it performs a semi-annual rebalancing. In doing so, it uses a full replication method, and the whole process results in a portfolio turnover of 57% (last FY), certainly not negligible. Participating in the fund involves bearing an expense ratio of 0.15% after fee waiver (guaranteed until 2027). Fees that are still well covered by the dividend yield, which today is 1.05%, with dividends distributed quarterly.

SPHQ was launched in 2005, so it has a decidedly broad track record for comparison purposes. An element that makes it a highly appreciated fund among quality factor-based ETFs; not surprisingly, in the recent period, it has benefited from very significant growth compared to the previous AUM values reported in the prospectus (April 2025: ~$12.4B).

What Does SPHQ Do?

What deserves further analysis is the selection strategy of the reference index. The quality factor in SPHQ follows three fundamental metrics to define a quality score:

  1. Return on Equity (ROE)
  2. Accruals Ratio (lower = better quality)
  3. Financial Leverage Ratio (For Financials and Real Estate, only ROE and leverage are used).

Then, the securities are weighted by Market Cap × Quality Score while maintaining the following constraints:

  • max 5% per security (or 20× its market cap weight in the parent index)
  • max 40% per sector
  • min 0.05% per security

At this point, the portfolio is set and a semi-annual rebalance is performed (June and December).

A process that is not trivial, especially on the side of the accruals ratio, a metric often overlooked by most commercial quality ETFs. Low accruals are correlated with substantial alpha in the literature. In fact, it is a metric that in the selection process is not used for financials and real estate securities, which are evaluated with a different method.

Yet, it results in a lower return than the S&P 500, why?

We notice that the performance spread began to form in 2025, and the reason is that the S&P 500 rally was predominantly driven by high beta and AI megacaps. In fact, the chart clearly shows the divergence only in the YTD.

Now many might repeat: “SPHQ has returned less than the S&P 500 since 2005, so it’s not worth it.” In reality, it could also be read in the opposite sense, like 2005-2025 is the most favorable cycle in history for tech megacaps, not for the quality factor. And yet the annualized return was still 9.60% versus 10.94% for the S&P 500. In the case of a sector rotation, then, how much could having a quality filter on your holdings help?

Let’s try to give an answer.

Who Is SPHQ For?

The ETF presents itself as a quality-oriented solution, and in common language, this is often associated with a strong ability to participate in market uptrends while also greatly limiting volatility, like a defensive or high-dividend ETF. And while on one hand the SA risk grade is good (B-), thanks to a slightly lower standard deviation and drawdown compared to a classic SPY, there are also other factors to consider.

Indeed, be careful, SPHQ is not a Low Volatility ETF. Its edge comes from:

  • sustainable ROE
  • low accruals = real earnings
  • leverage under control

The Quality factor is more profitable than the low-volatility factor: in fact, SPHQ beats classic defensive ETFs.

So, to sum up, SPHQ does not position itself as a portfolio substitute for defensive or low-volatility solutions. At the same time, it is not a high beta version of the S&P 500. This makes it less suitable to contribute as a satellite solution perhaps compared to an already US stock–oriented portfolio, especially if one is looking for high beta solutions (but the same applies to those seeking low beta solutions). Rather, it is a choice that stems from a “core” asset allocation logic, but that when needed can also become tactical, especially in certain market phases or during moments of particularly relevant and widespread macro rotation.

How Is SPHQ Built?

The ETF contains 100 stocks, all U.S. The top 10 positions represent about 39.31% of the portfolio, and the weighted average market capitalization is $420 billion. A figure that confirms SPHQ’s identity as an ETF focused on high-quality large caps.

A characteristic that is also confirmed in the sector distribution of the ETF, organized as follows:

This can be seen from the very first and most evident comparison: Mastercard and Visa collectively represent 8.5% of the portfolio, an anomaly considering that the distribution contains no banks. Likewise, GE at 4.31% is another strong anomaly. Then we notice the simultaneous presence of Coca-Cola, Costco and Procter & Gamble, an element that signals an implicit anti-cyclical filter. This together with Caterpillar at 3.26%, another “non-intuitive” choice for a quality ETF.

Looking at valuations, today the Price/Earnings (P/E) ratio is 25.61 while the Forward P/E is 22.29. The implicit ~13% discount relative to the current P/E means that analysts expect earnings growth or margin stability, considering a P/B of 15.79, much higher than the S&P 500. And this is an interesting element because the combination of a high P/B but a P/E that is not extremely high means that earnings are very high relative to invested capital. And this is natural, and becomes especially clear when attention shifts to the average Return on Equity, today at 54.25%, which is extremely high. Both compared to the S&P 500 average (~20%) and to competing quality ETFs (the QUAL ETF sits around 30–35%).

Peer comparison

Trying to reproduce the most comprehensive peer table possible, I selected these ETFs:

And the first evidence that stands out is that SPHQ manages to maintain a higher total return if we widen the time horizon to 10 years.

And the reason could lie precisely in its ability to select quality through its quality profitability-driven methodology (ROE + accruals + leverage). This gives SPHQ a competitive ROE of ~54%, which makes the selected companies more competitive in the long run following the logic of earning compound interest.

If attention shifts to the short term, 3Y performance or less, ETFs more tech-oriented like JQUA, FQAL, with greater weight in Nvidia, Meta and Microsoft naturally performed better, for the reasons mentioned above. But even here, I want to reiterate that these are minimal overperformances and short-term deviations.

Risks

First of all, I would like to emphasize an element to remember: SPHQ is not a low-volatility ETF, its beta is close to 0.9–0.95. It moves similarly to the S&P 500, and drawdowns are not really mitigated. It must therefore be clarified that “quality” does not equal “protection from bearish phases.”

But the risk may also consist in underperformance during positive phases. Note that, quality does not equal momentum, and if the rally of big tech and AI continues, SPHQ risks underperforming. In the end, the quality filter can exclude stocks that the market rewards heavily. And when does this happen? When there is a sector rotation toward high beta, when the market anticipates very aggressive rate cuts, and in moments of strong earnings dispersion.

Pros & Cons

To conclude, I think SPHQ has some fairly evident advantages and distinctive traits:

  1. Extremely high structural quality (ROE > 50%) and rigorous selection
  2. Better risk/return profile than the peer group
  3. Balanced composition: selected tech and defensive industrials

Of course, it would be irresonsible not to list the disadvantages as well:

  1. Underweight technology, which leads to a greater risk of underperforming in tech-driven rallies
  2. Overweight industrials, which in turn leads to a higher stagnation risk until the next rebalance
  3. And in the end, drawdowns similar to the S&P 500 (not a true defensive ETF).

This article answers these three main questions about SPHQ:

  • What type of quality metrics does SPHQ look at to determine stock selection?
  • How has SPHQ performed compared to other quality factor ETFs and the market as a whole?
  • How often is SPHQ rebalanced and does the rebalancing lead to higher turnover?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: American Outdoor POS To Sales Gap Widens, Potentially Signaling A Bullwhip Ahead
data.description:
American Outdoor Brands, Inc. (AOUT) reported fiscal Q2 '26 results, with challenging trends that follow the results seen in Q1 '26, adjusted for channel shifts because of tariffs.

Sales are down MSD when adjusted, with e-commerce leading the decrease. It seems retailers are managing inventory tightly, with POS doing better. The situation is not expected to improve throughout the year. Given the company's large operating leverage, margins will halve compared to last year's.

Analyzing the situation from an average of last year and this one, we can expect the company to generate $5/8 million in adjusted pre-tax income. I do not think a market cap of around $100 million reflects this earnings power, and therefore continue to think the name is not attractive, and maintain a Hold rating.

Q2 '26 results

AOUT's results for fiscal Q2 '26 were mixed, for several reasons.

On the one hand, we have a challenging trend in sales. The results show sales down 5% YoY, in what is already the strong seasonal period for AOUT. These results show a paper improvement from Q1 '26's results, which had a very negative mid-teens decline in sales, but that reflected a shift of orders to Q4 '25 to avoid tariffs. When adjusted for that shift, the company said at the time, Q1 '26 sales would have been down about 5% too. That is, Q2 '26 shows a continuation of the decreasing trend underlying Q1 '26 results. Furthermore, based on the company's guidance for FY26 (Q2 '26 call), sales will be down 5%, again after adjustments for the shift (-13/14% otherwise). That is, the company expects this trend to continue throughout the year.

On the other hand, the company claims that POS trends have been positive in both quarters (+4% in Q2 '26, not detailed but positive in Q1 '26) and that the gap with sales comes from retailers managing their inventories more tightly than before, because they want to avoid being over-inventoried in an uncertain consumer power context, and because consumption patterns are changing across different income segments, leading to adjustments on product lines, weights, assortment, etc.

This is particularly true, it seems, for the e-commerce segment, which is down 24% YTD versus the traditional segment, down 9% (both unadjusted for the shift). It seems the main culprit is Amazon (AOUT does not name it but says "the world's largest online retailer" on its 10-Q). Unfortunately, Amazon's (AMZN) inventory data is not as detailed and does not show a decrease in the aggregate.

The key question going forward, then, is whether the company's POS (according to the call, they have POS visibility on 60% of revenues) will eventually become the indicator for retailers' restocking, potentially with a bullwhip effect.

On the margin side, the company has shown significant operating leverage, particularly under $200 million in revenues yearly as today, as mentioned in their presentation (page 24). This led Q1 '26 margins to decrease significantly, posting adjusted EBITDA losses versus a small positive in Q1 '25. For Q2 '26, however, the situation is not that bad. Q2 '26 EBITDA margins were down 1pp (to 11.3%) versus Q2 '25, mainly via the reduction of OpEx, ~$1.8 million YoY (~$600 thousand in R&D, ~$600 thousand in S&M, ~$600 thousand in G&A), to compensate for a loss in gross profitability of almost $3 million.

Going forward, however, the company continues to guide for a bad year in terms of margins, expecting adjusted EBITDA in the 4/4.5% range, compared to 8% last year (which, conversely, had a sales boost from the tariff pre-ordering).

In terms of products or business developments, there were no specific announcements. The company won a prize for its very innovative Cladwell Copter (presentation, page 29), but there were no announcements of new products during the call.

Going forward and valuation

AOUT does not expect to be profitable on a GAAP basis, and only moderately on a cash basis this year.

It has guided sales down 13/14% versus last year's ~$220 million, implying $190 million in sales ($210 million if adjusted for the early shipments in FY25).

With an adjusted EBITDA margin expectation of 4/4.5%, it will generate adjusted EBITDA of about $8/9 million. The company has no debt, so it pays no interest, and will spend $4/5 million in CAPEX for the year, plus $6 million in stock-based compensation, or SBC (TTM). If we consider SBC an expense (I do), then the company cannot cover expenses this year. If we consider D&A of about $13 million per year, it will be GAAP unprofitable.

Still, on a cash basis, it should be positive, with potentially a slight cash generation of $3/4 million before working capital, albeit at the cost of dilution. The company's cash runway should be fine, given that it has $3 million in cash, no debt, and access to liquidity for $75 million in a credit line.

If we consider the above, then AOUT at ~$100 million market cap is definitely not attractive. However, the company has operating leverage, and this year's sales might not be fully representative of the average.

If we consider that $210 million is the average sales between this year and last year, as indicated by the company, we could expect an EBITDA margin closer to 7% (last year's 8%). In that case, adjusted EBITDA would be $15 million, which, after SBC and CAPEX, would result in about $5 million in pre-tax adjusted income.

Still not enough to justify a $100 million valuation, in my opinion, but better.

As commented in my previous post on AOUT, the upside and downside risks for the company lie in operational leverage. It claims that above $200 million in revenues, it can generate 30% marginal EBITDA profits. That implies that by $230 million, from our example above, it can generate $21 million in adjusted EBITDA and $11 million in pre-tax adjusted income. A 10% jump in sales generates a 100% jump in pre-tax adjusted income. Conversely, if sales decrease from $190 million this year, we could easily see adjusted EBITDA margins evaporate at sales maybe 5/10% lower than today. Although the financial/liquidity position is not tight, the company cannot simply extend its credit facility forever while unprofitable on an adjusted EBITDA basis.

Overall, I find that the company's stock price today tilts bullish, incorporating more of the positive scenario (return to normal and then some more). I would prefer a more neutral or even negative scenario incorporated, and therefore believe AOUT is not attractive, and maintain a Hold rating.

data.title: Tennant Company Will Clean Up
data.description:
Back in early September, one company that I decided to take another look at was Tennant Company (TNC). This is a fascinating enterprise, at least to me because not only is it one of the oldest companies out there, having been founded back in 1870, but also because of its overall business model. The firm produces and sells manual and autonomous mechanized cleaning equipment that is used by industrial and commercial clients. It also makes other things such as detergent free cleaning technologies, aftermarket parts and consumables, and more. The firm provides certain maintenance and repair services, and it even offers up financing, rental, and leasing programs for customers. There are other things as well, but you get the idea. At the end of the day, this is a large player with over 40,000 customers in over 100 different countries.

Leading up to that point in early September, I had been bullish about the business. This was even though top-line and bottom-line results for the enterprise had been worsening. And it was based on the company's valuation, both on an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms. Unfortunately, the market has disagreed with my assessment, pushing the stock down 12.2% since that article was published. By comparison, the S&P 500 is up 5.1%. As disappointing as this is, however, I maintain my bullish stance. This is a quality company that should continue to grow in the long run. And when you add on top of that its valuation, a bullish assessment just makes sense.

Shares Will Clean Up

The only new data that we currently have regarding Tennant Company covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue amounted to $303.3 million. That's a decline of 4% compared to the $315.8 million the company reported a year earlier. To really understand this drop, however, we need to dig deeper. The real weak spot, as the chart below illustrates, would be volume. This took an 8.2% hit during the quarter compared to the same time last year.

According to management, volume declines were prevalent in North America. Management chalked this up to what it called the lapping of a "significant backlog reduction benefit" that the company achieved the previous year. What this means, in short, is that in 2024, management worked hard to bring the backlog down, knowing that it would be essentially front-loading revenue at the expense of the future. This is not all that surprising to me. During and shortly after the COVID-19 pandemic, there was significant investment in anything hardware related. Companies across every industry that I am aware of made heavy investments because of a surge in demand as the economy reopened. It appears to me as though Tennant Company was no exception to this and was, in fact, a beneficiary of this trend.

By comparison, other regions performed much better. The closest to the Americas' region in terms of volume declines ended up being the Asia Pacific region, with a drop of 6.4% when it came to overall organic sales. Management blamed this on reduced commercial equipment volume in China and industrial equipment volume in South Korea. I know for a fact that China's economy has been slowing down. So none of that is surprising. South Korea, meanwhile, has been embroiled in the last couple of years in political crises, and it is suffering from significant population decline. So that's also not terribly shocking. By comparison, the EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) regions for the company performed much better, with sales actually rising 5.5%. But this was only because of foreign currency fluctuations. Actual organic sales dipped, but only slightly, with a decline of 0.4%, thanks to lower volumes in some of its markets. But in others, like the UK and Southern Europe, volumes improved.

The good news is that some of the pain from a volume standpoint was offset by other changes. As an example, higher prices that management was able to implement pushed revenue up by 2.8%. Some of this was because of tariffs that management decided to push on to its customers. However, the company also mentioned that the beginning of the year's price actions played a big role in this improvement also. We also saw a 1.4% benefit on top of this when it came to foreign currency fluctuations.

As revenue declined, profitability for the company also worsened. But this requires context. Actual GAAP net profits contracted from $20.8 million to $14.9 million. But if we adjust for certain one-time items, we actually get an increase from $26.6 million to $27.3 million. The company benefited from a slightly higher gross profit margin as management made sure to raise prices by more than just tariff increases. But that's not all. During the quarter, the company was able to add back higher ERP modernization costs that ended up being $2.4 million above what they were the previous year. They were also able to add back a $4 million legal contingency cost that involved a legal settlement that management had to pay out involving its intellectual property. And finally, during the most recent quarter, there was a $1 million restructuring-related expense as management continues to focus on certain strategic initiatives and long-term objectives that management has put into place.

Other profitability metrics for the company were approved. Operating cash flow did admittedly fall from $30.7 million to $28.7 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $39.4 million to $44 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $47.9 million to $49.8 million. In the chart above, you can also see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year.

This is where things do look a little different. Just like with what we saw for the third quarter, volume declines were problematic here. But the company did not benefit from as high a price increase on the goods that it sells. That boosted sales by only 1.5%. And foreign currency fluctuations added nothing to the top line. As a result of these items, profitability for the company worsened across the board. This is certainly discouraging, to say the least.

Management does expect some weakness to continue throughout the rest of this year. They said, for instance, that organic revenue should fall by between 3% and 5%. However, the firm does expect a very small improvement in its EBITDA margin, which would likely translate to EBITDA of $202.5 million. And if we project our results based on what we have seen already for the year, we would likely get adjusted net profits of $110.4 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $130.4 million.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These multiples aren't fantastic, but they certainly aren't bad. Relative to other firms that have similarities to it, Tennant Company is also attractively priced. In the table below, I compared it to five such enterprises. On both a price-to-earnings basis and on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, Tennant Company ended up being the cheapest of the group. Meanwhile, on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, only one of the five businesses ended up coming in cheaper.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Tennant Company 12.1 10.3 7.3
Mueller Industries (MLI) 16.5 16.4 11.6
Mayville Engineering Company (MEC) 29.4 5.5 13.6
SPX Technologies (SPXC) 46.1 33.8 23.1
The Timken Company (TKR) 19.3 10.4 9.4
Parker-Hannifin (PH) 30.7 29.3 23.1

In the short term, we should expect some weakness. But long term, I am confident in the company's potential. Not only is the stock cheap. Also, management is working on some very interesting initiatives. For instance, the firm is actively growing its AMR robotics business, with year-to-date sales up 9% and unit volumes rising by 25%. This is thanks to certain product lines like its X4 and X6 ROVR offerings, as well as major strategic customer wins globally. Another example would be the T360 mid-sized walk-behind scrubber that the company launched during the third quarter of this year. And on top of all of this, the company continues to see the successful rollout of its ERP system, with the Asia Pacific region experiencing the first go-live for this technology. The goal here that management has is to improve decision-making, to deliver better customer experiences, to boost cybersecurity, and to prepare the company to deploy AI offerings in the future. This kind of innovation has the potential to add significant upside to shareholders if it ultimately bears fruit.

Takeaway

As things stand, I must say that I am quite optimistic about Tennant Company. Yes, it is true that recent financial performance has been mixed and perhaps even slightly disappointing. But on the whole, this is a fascinating company that is doing reasonably well in a very tough environment. Shares are cheap, and the innovations that management continues to invest in should create even more value in the long run. Added all together, and I believe that maintaining Tennant Company as a "Buy" candidate is the right move.
